narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and choice to study full-time. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students, estimated an 80% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first try—this high risk led to the rejection of your application to protect both you and the school's outcomes. The decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the lower part of your class (around the 2nd decile, well below the typical 5th or 6th), strongly signaling to the model a heightened risk of bar failure compared to most applicants. Your Year 1 decile, also in the 2nd spot versus the average 5th or 6th, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor pushing toward that prediction. Even your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, lower than the usual 3.2, added to the model's worries about your academic trajectory. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 36—close to the average of 36.7—worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success, much like it does for stronger applicants. Your middle-family income bracket played a small role in raising the risk slightly, while opting for full-time study helped pull it back a bit, aligning with the 93% of students who choose that path. Overall, your lower early-year rankings stood out as bigger red flags than what we see in typical admitted students, whose deciles often hit the middle or higher. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman leader in law—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your deciles and GPA, perhaps seeking targeted tutoring or mentorship tailored to students like you. Reapply after strengthening those academics, as even small gains there could shift your predicted odds dramatically. We're rooting for your success and encourage exploring other schools or pathways that value your unique journey. Keep pushing—you've got this.
